title: Smallest Park

jokes.text: So, you're saying you want me to choose a new font? Yes, essentially, I'd like you to choose a new font.
jokes.setup: Tom is asked to redesign the entire Parks Department logo and visual brand
jokes.punchline: Leslie confirms it's essentially just choosing a new font
jokes.explanation: Solid deflation joke - builds up the grandiose redesign concept then reveals it's just font selection

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: So you want me to redesign the entire Parks Department logo and visual brand?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Yes! A complete overhaul. New colors, new fonts, new everything. Make it modern, make it pop!

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Okay, so... what exactly are we talking about here? Like, how different?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, essentially just choose a new font.

jokes.text: Jerry, you're number three. There's only two of us.
jokes.setup: Leslie assigns Jerry as number three on the font project
jokes.punchline: Jerry points out there are only two people total
jokes.explanation: Perfect absurdist logic and character moment - Leslie's overly complex hierarchies meet reality

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Jerry, you're number three on the font project.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: But Leslie, there are only two people total.

jokes.text: It is point-zero-zero-zero zero-zero-three square miles.
jokes.setup: Leslie describes creating an amazing, awe-inspiring park
jokes.punchline: The park is comically tiny - 0.000003 square miles
jokes.explanation: Great contrast between Leslie's grandiose vision and reality - the specific decimal precision makes it funnier

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I've created an amazing, awe-inspiring park. It's 0.000003 square miles.

jokes.text: But guess what, Terryville sucks old car tires and so does Martin Luther... No. He does not suck old car tires.
jokes.setup: Leslie gets competitive about beating Terryville's park record
jokes.punchline: She almost insults MLK Jr. before catching herself
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe moment showing Leslie's competitive nature overriding her better judgment

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm going to break Terryville's park record. I don't care if I have to work twenty-four hours a day, seven days a week. I will not rest until this park is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, you need to sleep.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Sleep is for people without vision, Ben. Sleep is for quitters and... and... people who aren't as driven as Martin Luther King Jr.— I mean, people who DON'T have dreams. Like MLK. He had dreams. Good dreams.

jokes.text: Oh, I did not graduate college because I did not 'attend it.'
jokes.setup: Discussion about Andy taking college classes
jokes.punchline: Andy's matter-of-fact explanation for not graduating
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Andy's innocent logic - the air quotes on 'attend it' sell the delivery

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I went to college for two and a half years. I got pretty close.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I would've graduated, but I ran out of money. Then I transferred to another school, but I ran out of money there too. Then I got really sick one semester, so I dropped out.

jokes.text: Horizons are dumb. Never broaden your horizons.
jokes.setup: Ron suggests Andy should broaden his horizons with education
jokes.punchline: April flatly rejects the concept of broadening horizons
jokes.explanation: Pure April - anti-intellectual stance delivered with complete confidence

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: Of all my coworkers, he is one of a small number whom I do not actively root against.
jokes.setup: Ron talking about valuing education for Andy
jokes.punchline: Ron's version of high praise is not actively rooting against someone
jokes.explanation: Classic Ron - his emotional restraint makes this backhanded compliment perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I've always said, if you want something, you have to work for it. Even education.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That's why I'm not actively rooting against you, Andy.

jokes.text: There I go, getting all sappy.
jokes.setup: Ron just gave what for him was an emotional statement about Andy
jokes.punchline: He considers not actively rooting against someone as 'getting sappy'
jokes.explanation: Perfect capper to the previous joke - Ron's self-awareness about his emotional distance

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I care about Andy. There, I said it.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm getting sappy.

jokes.text: The only good font is the Sopranos one, where the R is a pistol.
jokes.setup: Tom and Jerry discussing font choices for the Parks logo
jokes.punchline: April's only font preference is based on violence imagery
jokes.explanation: Perfectly April - dismisses the whole discussion but has a darkly specific preference

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: We need to pick a font for the new Parks logo. What do you think, Jerry?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, I think we should go with something clean and modern. Maybe Helvetica?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I only like fonts that look like they could kill me.

jokes.text: I'm more of a Steve Harvey. I dream big, I shake things up, and I always wear dope suits.
jokes.setup: Tom contrasting himself with Jerry's boring government drone approach
jokes.punchline: Tom compares himself to Steve Harvey with this specific list of qualities
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Tom's grandiose self-image with a random celebrity comparison

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm like Steve Harvey. I got the mustache, I got the charm, I got the gravitas.

jokes.text: I barely haven't even ever seen a gor-tar.
jokes.setup: Andy pretending to be a beginner at guitar despite clearly being experienced
jokes.punchline: His fake ignorance includes mispronouncing 'guitar' as 'gor-tar'
jokes.explanation: The double negative and mispronunciation perfectly capture Andy's attempt at deception

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm just learning the gor-tar. Still pretty new to it, you know?

jokes.text: Guess what. I was secretly an undercover rock star this whole time.
jokes.setup: Andy has been pretending to be a guitar beginner
jokes.punchline: He 'reveals' his secret in the most obvious way possible
jokes.explanation: Andy's innocent approach to 'deception' - he can't help but reveal himself

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, I have a confession to make. I've been pretending this whole time.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm actually really good at guitar.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I've been playing since I was a kid.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: We know.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What? How?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Andy, you've been playing 'Wonderwall' perfectly for the last twenty minutes.

jokes.text: For what it's worth, I think you would make an incredible brunette.
jokes.setup: Random comment to a woman, presumably the guitar instructor
jokes.punchline: Ron's oddly specific and unexpected compliment
jokes.explanation: Classic Ron non-sequitur - his attempts at compliments are always slightly off

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: You have the slenderest fingers I've ever seen. It's like you don't have any bones in them. They're like beautiful, beautiful worms.

jokes.text: So, please join us for the grand opening of Pawnee's smallest park on November 12th, 2070.
jokes.setup: Leslie explaining her plan to drag out the park project
jokes.punchline: She schedules the opening 50+ years in the future
jokes.explanation: Great escalation of Leslie's desperate plan - the specific far-future date makes it funnier

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm going to schedule the park opening so far in the future that by the time it actually opens, I'll have already accomplished all my other goals and won't care about this one anymore.

jokes.text: Well, there's barely room for an actual basketball, much less a court.
jokes.setup: Resident worried about basketball courts attracting undesirables
jokes.punchline: Ben points out the park is too small for even a basketball
jokes.explanation: Solid reality-check joke highlighting the absurd scale of the park

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: The park is too small for a basketball court. I mean, you'd literally just have a basketball rolling around in a parking lot.

jokes.text: One of the most significant bummers of my lifetime.
jokes.setup: Professor explains they won't use actual lasers in laser class
jokes.punchline: Andy treats this as a major life disappointment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy - his childlike disappointment escalated to dramatic proportions

dialogue.speaker: Professor
dialogue.line: We won't be using actual lasers in this class.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What? No actual lasers? This is the worst day of my life.

jokes.text: Today I'm a Beverly Hills plastic surgeon. And the Parks Department is a wrinkly old housewife in her early 30s.
jokes.setup: Tom describing his plans to redesign the Parks Department
jokes.punchline: His oddly specific and insulting analogy
jokes.explanation: Classic Tom grandiosity with an unexpectedly specific and problematic comparison

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm going to redesign the Parks Department from the ground up. We need to be sleek, efficient, and profitable. Think of it like this: right now, we're a Pontiac Aztek. Ugly, nobody wants it, and it smells like desperation and broken dreams.

jokes.text: I once hosted a forum about a new bike rack that lasted seven hours. Now, when I need these people to complain, they're done in 45 minutes?
jokes.setup: Leslie's meeting ends quickly instead of dragging on
jokes.punchline: She's frustrated people aren't complaining enough when she needs them to
jokes.explanation: Great irony - Leslie usually can't get people to stop talking, now she can't get them to start

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait, that's it? We're done? But we haven't even gone through the zoning variance requests, or the noise ordinance amendments, or... anyone? Does anyone have any concerns? Complaints? Anything?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: No? Really? This is so frustrating. I need you people to complain more. How am I supposed to solve problems if nobody tells me what the problems are?

jokes.text: Why would I take a class on something that's already happened?
jokes.setup: Looking at Civil War class option
jokes.punchline: Andy's logic about studying history
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy logic - completely missing the point of studying history in the most innocent way

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Why would I take a Civil War class? I already know how it ends. The North wins.

jokes.text: I want to be burned at the stake.
jokes.setup: Professor describes Joan of Arc's courage facing execution
jokes.punchline: Andy completely misses the point and wants the experience
jokes.explanation: Beautifully wrong response - Andy's enthusiasm for completely missing inspirational messages

dialogue.speaker: Professor
dialogue.line: Joan of Arc showed incredible courage facing execution, knowing she was right.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: That sounds amazing. Do you think they'd let me do that?

jokes.text: Andy Dwyer will be taking Women's Lasers. Women's Studies. Sorry. God, I cannot stop thinking about lasers.
jokes.setup: Andy earlier disappointed about laser class, now choosing Women's Studies
jokes.punchline: He mishears it as 'Women's Lasers' and can't let go of laser obsession
jokes.explanation: Good callback to earlier laser disappointment - Andy's one-track mind creates the perfect mishearing

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, Women's Studies? That sounds amazing!

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: It is. It's a real class.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Women's... Studies... Women's Lasers!

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: No, Studies.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: That's what I said. Women's Lasers.

jokes.text: Andy Dwyer will be taking Women's Lasers. Women's Studies. Sorry. God, I cannot stop thinking about lasers.
jokes.setup: Andy choosing Women's Studies class
jokes.punchline: He keeps conflating it with his laser obsession
jokes.explanation: Good callback showing Andy's one-track mind about lasers

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm taking Women's Studies.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Why?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: To learn about women and their studies.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That's not... never mind.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Did you know that women invented the laser? No wait, that was lasers. Women invented... the study of lasers?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Get out of my office.

jokes.text: I do, Joe. Yeah, that's how I know just how high this conspiracy goes.
jokes.setup: Resident asks if Leslie works for the government regarding park problems
jokes.punchline: Leslie confirms but frames herself as a whistleblower exposing government conspiracy
jokes.explanation: Great escalation - Leslie's desperation leads her to become a conspiracy theorist about her own project

dialogue.speaker: Resident
dialogue.line: Wait, you work for the government?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I do, but I'm also a whistleblower exposing a massive government conspiracy.

jokes.text: Well, guess what, Jerry. I'm a dreamer. Okay? I dream big. Deal with it.
jokes.setup: Jerry tries to redirect Tom back to the simple font task
jokes.punchline: Tom's defensive and grandiose response
jokes.explanation: Classic Tom defensiveness and self-importance. The 'deal with it' is appropriately bratty for his character.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Tom, we really just need you to pick a simple font for the document.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Simple? SIMPLE?! I am not a simple man, Jerry. I am a complex tapestry of ambition, style, and unbridled charisma. My font choices are a reflection of my soul. I will not be confined to your pedestrian notions of simplicity.

jokes.text: So, I don't think we should work together anymore.
jokes.setup: Ben explaining Leslie steamrolls him in their partnership
jokes.punchline: His public declaration that they shouldn't work together
jokes.explanation: More dramatic than comedic, but the public awkwardness creates cringe humor

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie steamrolls me in our partnership. I tried to tell her we shouldn't work together, but she just kept talking over me.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: So I made a public declaration about it.

jokes.text: Hello, ma'am, who is just as equal as men.
jokes.setup: Andy trying to register for Women's Studies
jokes.punchline: His awkward attempt at feminist-appropriate greeting
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy - his well-meaning but clumsy attempt to be politically correct

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Hey ladies, I'm here to register for Women's Studies. I'm Andy Dwyer, and I'm a feminist.

dialogue.speaker: Professor
dialogue.line: That's great. Have you taken any prerequisite courses?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: No, but I've watched a lot of Lifetime movies, and I'm pretty sure I know what's going on.

jokes.text: I would like one ticket for Women.
jokes.setup: Andy trying to register for Women's Studies class
jokes.punchline: He treats it like buying a ticket to an event called 'Women'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Andy moment - his complete misunderstanding of how college registration works

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'd like to register for Women's Studies, please.

dialogue.speaker: Registrar
dialogue.line: That class is full.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh man. Do you know when the next Women's Studies is? I've been trying to catch Women for like three months.

jokes.text: I'm going to beg for money on the street like a drug addict.
jokes.setup: Andy can't afford college and won't take April's parents' money
jokes.punchline: His immediate jump to street begging as solution
jokes.explanation: Good Andy escalation to the most dramatic solution

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I can't take April's parents' money. That's not who I am.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm gonna start a street begging business. I'll be like a regular beggar, but with better jokes.

jokes.text: You are a massive, enormous, runaway steamroller, with no brakes and a cement brick on the gas pedal.
jokes.setup: Ann finally tells Leslie she's a steamroller
jokes.punchline: The increasingly elaborate description of Leslie as an out-of-control steamroller
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the steamroller metaphor - the specific details make Ann's frustration funnier

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Leslie, you're a steamroller.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What? A steamroller?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Yes. You just steamroll over people and their feelings.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I do not steamroll people!

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: You're like a steamroller that's lost its brakes, barreling down a hill, crushing everything in its path, and you don't even notice because you're too busy planning the next thing.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That is not fair.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: You're a steamroller that's on fire, Leslie. A flaming steamroller with a megaphone, screaming about your vision, and anyone who disagrees gets flattened and set ablaze.

jokes.text: Well, then, you should call me a bulldozer rather than a steamroller.
jokes.setup: Ann calls Leslie a steamroller who pushes too hard
jokes.punchline: Leslie's practical correction about which machine would be more accurate
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie - even when accepting criticism, she wants to get the technical details right

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: You're a steamroller, Leslie. You push too hard.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Actually, a steamroller would be a more accurate description if I were flattening things, which I'm not. I'm more of a... a very efficient organizer who occasionally gets ahead of herself.

jokes.text: The roll-y thing on the front of the machine. What do you call that? Roller? Probably.
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to complete her steamroller metaphor
jokes.punchline: She doesn't know what to call the roller on a steamroller
jokes.explanation: Leslie's attempt at a poetic metaphor falls apart due to lack of mechanical knowledge

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm like a steamroller, just flattening everything in my path, and I'm not going to stop until... until the... the roller thing... what do you call that?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: The roller?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Yes, thank you.

jokes.text: Heck, I still use my original ID card from my first day on the job.
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining his 30+ years of doing everything the same way
jokes.punchline: He shows his decades-old ID card (visual gag of young Jerry)
jokes.explanation: Visual gag setup - the contrast between current Jerry and his young photo would be the payoff

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I've been doing everything the same way for over 30 years.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Same clothes, same routines, same ID card photo.

jokes.text: Good news, son. You have just won a Ron Swanson scholarship.
jokes.setup: Andy needs money for college
jokes.punchline: Ron invents a scholarship named after himself on the spot
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron solution - practical generosity disguised as bureaucratic process

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Ron, I need help paying for college.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm going to create a scholarship in my name. The Ron Swanson Memorial Scholarship for Excellence in Woodworking and Breakfast Foods.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Ron, you're not dead.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Not yet.

jokes.text: One thing I promised myself when I buried gold in my backyard was that I'd never be a hoarder or a miser about it.
jokes.setup: Ron explaining his motivation for helping Andy financially
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of buried gold as if it's normal
jokes.explanation: Classic Ron - drops insane personal detail (buried gold) as casual backstory to reasonable principle

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm helping Andy because I believe in investing in people who show promise. It's practical, it's efficient, and frankly, I have more money than I could spend in ten lifetimes anyway. I've got at least three barrels of it buried in various locations around Pawnee.

jokes.text: She once made me eat an entire cheesecake at a potluck so she didn't look bad.
jokes.setup: Ann explaining Leslie can be difficult but she's loyal
jokes.punchline: Specific example of Leslie's steamroller behavior affecting Ann
jokes.explanation: Perfect example of Leslie's steamroller behavior - the absurd specificity makes it funnier

jokes.text: Thanks. But most of the credit goes to my number four, Jerry.
jokes.setup: Tom being praised for his retro logo idea
jokes.punchline: Jerry has somehow been demoted to number four
jokes.explanation: Good callback showing the continued degradation of Jerry's status

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Thank you, thank you. I'm glad you all appreciate my vision.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Tom, this is incredible. You've really outdone yourself.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I know. I'm basically a design genius at this point.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Actually, I came up with that logo back in 1987.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Nobody asked you, Jerry. You're number four now.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I was number three yesterday.

jokes.text: There is another option. We could just say, 'Screw it,' and do this thing for real.
jokes.setup: Leslie accepting Ben doesn't want to work together anymore
jokes.punchline: She immediately pivots to suggesting they date instead
jokes.explanation: Leslie's inability to accept defeat leads to romantic escalation - character-driven plot turn

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, I respect that. You don't want to work together anymore. I get it. So... do you want to go out sometime?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, no.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What? Come on, Ben. We have great chemistry. We make each other laugh.

jokes.text: That is extremely incorrect. I thought maybe it was.
jokes.setup: Andy giving a completely wrong answer in Women's Studies
jokes.punchline: His casual acceptance that he was completely wrong
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy - completely unbothered by being spectacularly wrong, treating learning as casual trial and error

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yeah, I have no idea what you're talking about. I'm just gonna go back to my desk and eat a tub of ice cream.

dialogue.speaker: Professor
dialogue.line: Mr. Dwyer, that's not even close to the correct answer.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know. But you know what? That's cool. Ice cream's delicious.

tmdb_overview: Leslie uses her new tourist attraction as a way to spend more time with Ben. Meanwhile, Ron and April encourage Andy to go to college, and Chris tasks Tom and Jerry with revamping the Parks and Rec logo.
insight: Ben's budget-cutting chaos escalates into 38 jokes—Parks' densest governmental absurdist episode.
